title: Why You Keep Messing Up on Negative Numbers (And How to Fix It)
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You understand algebra. You know the steps. But you keep getting the wrong answer. Why? Most of the time, it's negative signs. One tiny minus sign in the wrong place ruins the whole problem. Here's how to fix that habit forever.

The Number Line Is Your Friend

Visualize a number line. Negative numbers are to the left of zero. Positive numbers are to the right. When you add a negative number, you move left. When you subtract a negative number, you move right (because subtracting a negative is the same as adding a positive).

Draw a quick number line on scratch paper if you're unsure. It takes five seconds and prevents sign errors.

The Two Negatives Rule

Two negatives cancel out. They become positive. This is true for:
• Subtraction: 5 - (-3) = 5 + 3 = 8
• Multiplication: (-2) × (-4) = 8
• Division: (-10) ÷ (-2) = 5

An even number of negatives = positive. An odd number of negatives = negative. Count the negatives before you do anything else.

Rewrite Subtraction as Addition

Subtracting a number is the same as adding its opposite. Instead of 7 - 4, think 7 + (-4). This small shift helps you avoid forgetting the sign of the second term.

Practice rewriting every subtraction problem as addition for one week. It will become automatic.

Distribute the Negative Sign Fully

The most common algebra mistake: -(x + 3) = -x + 3. Wrong. The negative distributes to both terms: -(x + 3) = -x - 3.

Think of the negative sign as multiplying everything inside the parentheses by -1. Every term changes sign.

Check Your Work by Plugging In

After you solve an equation, plug your answer back into the original equation. Does it work? If 2x - 5 = 11 and you got x = 8, check: 2(8) - 5 = 16 - 5 = 11. Correct. If it doesn't work, you made a sign error somewhere.

This takes ten seconds and catches 90% of careless mistakes.

Common Negative Number Traps

Trap 1: Subtracting a larger number from a smaller one. 3 - 7 = -4, not 4. The answer is negative. Visualize the number line.

Trap 2: Squaring negatives. (-3)² = 9. The parentheses matter. -3² = -9 (the exponent only applies to the 3, not the negative). Always use parentheses.

Trap 3: Forgetting the negative in slope. If a line goes down as it goes right, the slope is negative. Double-check your sign before moving on.

A Simple Self-Check Routine

After every problem, ask yourself three questions:
1. Did I count the negatives correctly?
2. Did I distribute the negative to every term?
3. Did I check my answer by plugging it back in?

Do this for every problem for two weeks. By the end, checking signs will be automatic. Your algebra grades will improve immediately.
